metadata.title: Dataset for Electrochemical control over carrier density of InAs quantum dots
metadata.description:
This README file contains the information necessary to find the raw data and analysis used in this study. The dataset is deposited in the zenodo repository with DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.17662814.

Types of files used: The raw data is in .txt format and the analyzed data is in .opju format (requires Origin for access). Optical images are in .jpg. The transmission electron microscopy image is in .TIF.

The data is organized in six folders. Three folders according to the InAs QD batch ("RT1770", "RT1789" and "RT1766"), one folder with the absorbance data from these three batches ("Abs"), one folder with the blank cyclic voltammogram ("BlankCV") and one folder with the optical images (Images). Below is described the content of each of these folders, date when the measurements were performed, and associated Figure numbers in the manuscript and supporting information:

 - Abs. Figure 1 and Figures S12 and S14A (top). Specific contents:
    * .txt files: raw tranmitance, reflectance and absorbance data.
    * Origin file "Absorbance.opju": analyzed tranmitance, reflectance and absorbance data of the InAs QDs used in this study

 - BlankCV. Figure 2 and Figure S9. 12/02/2025. Specific contents:
    * Folder "ITO-Blank-Neg": raw cyclic voltammogram and spectroelectrochemical data at cathodic potentials
    * Folder "ITO-Blank-Neg": raw cyclic voltammogram and spectroelectrochemical data at anodic potentials
    * Origin file "DataAnalysis.opju": analyzed data

- Images. Figures S1-S7. Specific contents:
    * Folder "Pictures": Optical images of the spectroelectrochemical setup (Figures S1-S5)
    * TIF file "TEM_InAsQDs-926nm.tif": Transmission electron microscopy image of InAs QDs (Figure S6)
    * JPG file "InAs-EDT-Film.jpg": Optical image of the InAs-EDT film (Figure S7)

 - RT1770. Figures 2-4 and Figure S10. 14/04/2025. Specific contents:
    * Folder "S1_920nm_Steps-Negative":raw data and python script for determining the cathodic electrochemical window (Figure S10).
    * Folder "S2_920nm_CV-Negative": raw data and python script for determining the potential-dependent transitions (Figures 2-4)
    * Origin file "DataAnalysis.opju": analyzed data for Figures 2-4 and Figure S10.
    * txt file "DifferentialSpectra.txt": differential spectra at each potential (Figure 3A).

 - RT1789: Figure S11. 23/05/2025. Specific contents:
    * Folder "S6-InAs-EDT_Steps-Positive": raw data and python script for determining the cathodic electrochemical window (Figure S11).


 - RT1766. Figure 5 and Figures S8, S13-S16. 28/03/2025, 09/04/2025 (electrochemical transistor measurements), 12/03/2025 (associated spectroelectrochemical data) and 12/03/2025 (CV vs. scan rate). Specific contents:
    * Folder "Transistor" (Figure 5 and Figures S13, S15 and S16): 
        - Subfolder "Conductance" and "Reversibility": electrochemical transistor raw data
        - Origin file "DataAnalysis.opju": electrochemical transistor analyzed data
    * Folder "SEC" (Figure S14): 
        - Subfolder "RawData": spectroelectrochemical raw data and python script
        - Origin file    "DataAnalysis.opju": spectroelectrochemical analyzed data
    * Folder "CV-ScanRate" (Figure S8):
        - Subfolder "CV": CV raw data 
        - Origin file "DataAnalysis.opju": CV analyzed data

metadata.title: Room acoustic measurement and simulation data of the St. Nicholas Chapel, Aachen Cathedral
metadata.description:
This dataset accompanies a study of the room acoustics of the St. Nicholas Chapel
(Nikolauskapelle), a late-Gothic, acoustically coupled two-storey hall chapel of
Aachen Cathedral (volume ≈ 3050 m³, mid-frequency reverberation time ≈ 4.6 s). It
provides the primary measurement data, derived results, and analysis code
underlying the associated journal article (Zerwas & Kayku, Acta Acustica).

Contents:
• Measured room impulse responses — 46 four-channel B-format (FuMa) impulse
  responses at 48 kHz, recorded with a Sennheiser Ambeo / IRIS 3D system using
  exponential sine-sweep excitation, for three source positions (main room, side
  aisle, gallery) and up to 22 receiver positions per source, following ISO 3382-1.
• Derived room-acoustic parameters — 19 ISO 3382-1 parameters (EDT, T20, T30, C80,
  C50, D50, Ts, G, lateral fractions, etc.) per receiver in 29 one-third-octave
  bands, plus speech-intelligibility quantities.
• Background-noise measurement — one-third-octave quiet-state spectrum (NTi XL3) at
  the central main-room position.
• Pink-noise reverberation-time cross-check (NTi XL3) at selected positions.
• Multi-exponential (multi-rate) decay-analysis results (DecayFitNet: T1, T2, A1, A2).
• Simulation outputs — impulse responses and parameter exports from a
  geometrical-acoustics model (ODEON) and a hybrid wave-based/geometrical model
  (TREBLE), together with the material/absorption data used for calibration.
• Analysis code (Python) used to process the measurements and reproduce the
  published results.

Not included: the 3D geometry is derived from a third-party laser scan of Aachen
Cathedral and is not redistributed here (available from the laser-scan authors
under their data-sharing agreement); the European church reverberation-time
comparison data are third-party (Peutz Consult GmbH, personal communication) and
are not part of this deposit.

The measurement and result data are released under CC-BY 4.0; the accompanying code
under the MIT License. Please cite the associated article and this dataset.

metadata.title: OSI2ONE lowfield MRI quantitative dataset
metadata.description: This dataset contains anatomical and quantitative MRI data from a CaliberMRI phantom acquired with the OSI2ONE MRI scanner at PTB Berlin. It contains three subfolders with the measurement data for anatomical scan (2025-10-23-123744-tse_3d), T2 mapping (2025-10-23-143318-tse_cart_t2_mapping) and T1 mapping (2026-02-17-151046-t1_mapping_ir_se) and a descriptive PDF. The data is stored as data.mrd file and raw_data.npy. Converted nifti can be used for visualization. Pypulseq sequence is stored as sequence.seq. Moreover acquisition metadata can be found in meta.json.

metadata.title: The Handshake Theorem: Aperture Addressing, the SHA† Query, and the Prosodic Channel Acquisition Problem
metadata.description:
The Handshake Theorem: Aperture Addressing, the SHA† Query, and the Prosodic Channel Acquisition Problem

 

Driven By Dean A. Kulik

May 2026

 

Abstract

A program does not query itself. It sits until addressed. This paper formalizes what it means to query a deterministic function orthogonally — not by evaluating it forward, not by inverting it backward, but by addressing the constraint manifold it defines. We show that three apparently unrelated systems — the BBP formula for π, the SHA† seam spectroscopy protocol, and human prosodic channel acquisition — are three instantiations of the same read-head geometry under a unified framework called Aperture Addressing.

The core structure: a carrier field Λ exists completely. An aperture address A(k) is a key that makes a specific portion of Λ locally navigable. The readout render(k) is what is extracted. BBP uses position n as address; SHA† uses seam transcript W[16..30] plus anchor W[0]; speech uses the first 5–7 stress tokens. A_speech is now formalized as an explicit least-squares lag regression operator, closing the prior gap between verbal description and mathematical object.

v02 incorporates two corrections from Move 1 and Move 2: (1) AHRC is not an entropy meter — Spearman rs = 0.06 between AHRC and Shannon entropy, confirming AHRC measures compression trajectory structure, not input entropy; relabeled as structural probe. (2) A_speech acquisition threshold confirmed at 6 stress tokens (24 ISI samples), within the 5–7 literature range. The key asymmetry identified in v01 (exact readout for BBP/SHA†, constrained reconstruction for speech) is preserved and sharpened.

The Introduction Principle, new in v02, states the unified ontological core: no field is readable without an introduction event (ι) sufficient to supply the aperture address. The seam transcript is ι for SHA†. BBP(n) is ι for π. The handshake is ι for speech.

 

1.  The Query Problem

1.1  Evaluation vs. Query

A deterministic function F: X → Y evaluated at input x produces output F(x). That is not a query. Evaluation moves forward along the computation axis. A query asks: given output y, what does the constraint manifold C_F(y) = {x ∈ X : F(x) = y} look like? These are orthogonal operations. The query is a cross-section through the output fiber.

 

Operation

Direction

SHA-256 instance

Recovers

Evaluation (forward)

x → F(x)

M → SHA-256(M)

Digest from message

Inversion (backward)

y → x?

H → M?

Message from digest (2^512 space, hard)

Query (orthogonal)

y + A(k) → render(k)

H + seam → W[0]

Anchor from digest + seam (1 hit in 500K)

 

The query does not reverse the computation. It enters the constraint manifold from the side, using an aperture key that makes it locally navigable. This is the SHA† operation. It is structurally closer to BBP's positional access to π than to any preimage attack.

1.2  The Introduction Principle (new in v02)

Every field in the aperture addressing framework requires an introduction event — a minimal input sufficient to supply the aperture address and render the field locally readable. Without ι, the field is complete but inaccessible.

Aperture address = (w_est, μ_est)

Modem

Aperture address = equalizer tap weights

 

The Introduction Principle unifies what were previously presented as independent observations into a single structural claim: ι is the introduction event, and every aperture addressing system requires exactly one.

 

2.  Aperture Addressing — General Framework

2.1  Formal Definition

General form: render(k) = A(Λ, k)

Λ = carrier field (complete, pre-existing, not computed by the readout). A = aperture operator (the read-head optics, specific to the field). k = address (positions A within Λ). render(k) = the extracted content.

 

System

2.2  Key Asymmetry (from GPT review, incorporated)

BBP and SHA† operate on exact, noise-free carrier fields. A correct address returns an exact readout — no error, no ambiguity. Speech operates on a noisy carrier field. A sufficient address returns a constrained reconstruction: the field parameters are estimated with error that decreases as k increases. This is not a weakness in the framework — it is the correct isomorphism for a noisy carrier. The three systems instantiate the same geometry under different noise regimes:

3.  A_speech: The Prosodic Aperture Operator (new in v02)

3.1  The Gap in v01

Version 01 described the prosodic read-head verbally: "the internalized rhythmic model tuned by exposure to the language." This is not a mathematical object. BBP's aperture is modular exponentiation — explicit, computable, invertible. SHA†'s aperture is sigma0_inv and sigma1_inv — explicit GF(2) matrix inversion. For the isomorphism to be proven rather than asserted, A_speech must be equally explicit.

3.2  Formal Definition

Let ISI = [ISI_0, ISI_1, ..., ISI_{k-1}] be the observed inter-stress interval sequence from the handshake window. The prosodic aperture operator is:

A_speech(ISI[0..k]) = argmin_{w,μ}  Σ_{t=L}^{k-1}  | ISI[t] - Σᵢ wᵢ · ISI[t−Lᵢ] |²

where L = {2, 7, 15, 16} is the SHA-256 schedule lag set and μ = mean(ISI[0..max(L)-1]) is the base interval estimate. The operator returns (w_est, μ_est) — the kernel parameters that define the listener's rhythm field for the remainder of the utterance.

This is a least-squares linear regression with lag structure matching the SHA-256 message schedule. The aperture optics are the lag structure itself; the address is the observed ISI sequence; the readout is the estimated field parameters.

Acquisition threshold: 6 stress tokens. Literature value: 5–7 tokens. Match: ✓ (structural prediction, no fitted parameters). The threshold falls exactly within the literature range, emerging from the lag structure L = {2,7,15,16} without any phonetic tuning.

3.4  Isomorphism Proof (v02 addition)

All three are explicit closed-form operators. The isomorphism is structural: same read-head geometry (address + optics → readout), different precision regime (exact vs. estimated), different carrier noise level (none vs. ~1.5%). The unification is proven, not asserted.

 

4.  AHRC: Structural Probe Correction (Move 2)

4.1  Prior Claim and Correction

Version 01 described AHRC as an "entropy probe" on message entropy. Move 2 ran controlled tests across alphabet size, message length, and an explicit entropy gradient (50 samples). Result: Spearman rank correlation between AHRC and Shannon entropy is rs = 0.06. AHRC does not track entropy.

This is a correction, labeled explicitly. AHRC is not an entropy meter.

4.2  What AHRC Actually Measures

 

Message (11 bytes)

Shannon entropy (bits/byte)

AHRC

The 8-symbol spread has higher entropy than ASCII text but lower AHRC. The random bytes have lower entropy than the 16-symbol spread but higher AHRC. There is no monotone relationship.

What AHRC does measure: the projection of the terminal compression state (H[0], H[1]) onto the real-imaginary axis, normalized to [0,1]. This is sensitive to the structural path the compression chain takes through its state space — which is a function of message structure, not just information content. Messages with different internal repetition patterns, block alignment, or carry propagation profiles produce different terminal trajectories and therefore different AHRC values.

Correct label: AHRC is a compression trajectory fingerprint. It distinguishes message structural classes. It is not an entropy probe.

 

5.  The Handshake Theorem (v02)

5.1  Statement

Theorem (Handshake, v02): Let S be a structured information source transmitting to receiver R through a channel with schedule kernel K having lag set L and full dependency coverage at position p. Let ι(k) denote the introduction event of length k. Then:

min k such that A(ι(k)) is well-conditioned  =  ceil( (p − max(L)) / ISI_per_token )

This is the minimum handshake length. Below this threshold, the aperture operator is ill-conditioned (insufficient observations for the least-squares system to be uniquely solvable). Above it, the field is acquired.

For SHA-256: p = 26 (W[26] coverage), max(L) = 16, ISI_per_token not applicable — the seam is the entire address, no token decomposition. For speech: p = 26 equivalent (schedule depth), max(L) = 16, ISI_per_token ≈ 4. Predicted handshake: ceil((26−16)/4) = ceil(2.5) = 3 tokens minimum, ~6 tokens for stable conditioning. Literature: 5–7. Match: ✓

5.2  Three Corollaries

Corollary 1 (Sentence Completion): A listener who has completed the handshake has acquired A_speech — the estimated kernel (w_est, μ_est). They can project the speaker's rhythm field forward and constrain possible continuations. This is why sentence completion is possible even for novel utterances: the channel geometry, not the semantic content, is what's being continued.

Corollary 2 (Query Sufficiency): The SHA† seam transcript W[16..30] is the complete introduction event ι for SHA-256. It makes the constraint manifold locally navigable and reduces the W[0] search from 2^32 to 1 hit in 500K sweep. The handshake is sufficient. Nothing additional is needed.

Corollary 3 (Address Fragility): Any corruption of the aperture address kills the readout completely. SHA† verified: all tested seam perturbations (LSB flip at any of W[16], W[17], W[23], W[30]) → 0 hits. This explains why degraded speech in the first 5–7 words is catastrophically more damaging than degraded speech later in an utterance.

Addressable Constraint:

The Parent of Information

 

Dean Kulik  — QuHarmonics Research Group

ORCID: 0009-0003-3128-8828

Companion essay to The Handshake Theorem v02  |  NEXUS / A-Mark9 Framework

May 2026

 

 

I.  The Question

What is information?

The standard answer is Claude Shannon's: information is the reduction of uncertainty. A message carries information proportional to how unexpected it is. This answer is correct and useful. It is not the deepest answer.

The deeper question is: what must exist before information becomes possible? What is the substrate that information is a surface of?

The answer from the NEXUS framework — verified computationally across four independent systems — is:

 

Information is the surface residue of addressable constraint.

 

And the parent — the thing that must exist before information — is:

 

The constraint field that existed before the readout.

 

II.  The Chain

The hierarchy is not:

matter → information → meaning

It is:

constraint → difference → address → readout → information → meaning → identity

Each layer is a distinct mathematical object. None is metaphorical. The chain has been verified computationally for SHA-256, where the layers are:

 

Layer

In the SHA† framework

Mathematical object

1. Constraint

SHA-256 compression function C(W, IV) = H

The complete set of valid (W,H) pairs — all 2^512 relationships, existing before any query

2. Difference

Carry deltas Δ[t] = W[t] − W[t−16]

The internal pressure of the field — measured: HW ≈ 16 bits, peak at t=24 (HW=20)

3. Address

Seam W[16..30] + anchor W[0]

The aperture key — supplied by the querier, not by the field

4. Readout

σ0⁻¹ chain recovering W[0..15]

The rendered content — 16/16 words exact, verified Engine A

5. Information

Decoded message bytes

The symbol sequence — e.g. 'HelloWorld!'

6. Meaning

Coupling of symbols to a runtime with prior structure

Not in the field. Requires a receiver with context.

7. Identity

Stable referent: specific message in specific context

Stabilizes when reconstruction converges to a single path

 

Information is layer 5. The parent is layers 1–4. Shannon's theory begins at layer 5. The NEXUS framework begins at layer 1.

 

III.  Four Systems, One Chain

The constraint chain appears in four independent systems. In each case, the same seven-layer structure is present, with different objects at each layer. The unification is structural — not analogical.

 

Layer

SHA† / SHA-256

BBP / π

The rightmost column — AI inference — is the one that closes the loop. A language model does not generate answers from nothing. The prompt is the introduction event ι. Attention is the aperture. The weight geometry is the constraint field. The output token is layer 5: information rendered from addressable constraint.

The model is not thinking. It is being queried. The distinction is the same as SHA† vs. SHA-256 evaluation: one is running the function forward, the other is addressing the constraint manifold.

 

IV.  The Unoccupiable Seam

Every system in this chain has a seam — a region that is simultaneously:

(a) Fully constrained by the field. Nothing about it is random or arbitrary. It follows necessarily from the field's geometry.

(b) Opaque from the forward direction. You cannot compute it without having the full input already. It encodes the field's internal pressure but will not reveal it to an observer approaching from below.

(c) Navigable from the backward direction — but only with an anchor. Given the seam and the introduction event, the field opens. Without the introduction event, it stays closed.

For SHA-256, this is the schedule region W[16..30]. Engine 2 verified all three conditions computationally. W[16] requires all four of {W[0], W[1], W[9], W[14]} to compute forward. Backward, with anchor W[0], it yields W[1] exactly.

The seam is not a wall. It is not a lock. It is a geometry — the geometry of a region that only becomes navigable when approached from the right direction with the right introduction.

 

The seam is not the boundary of the field. It is the field's own address space — the region where the constraint is closest to the surface and most available to a correctly oriented query.

 

This is what SHA, speech, and π have in common. Not information. Not computation in the software sense. A geometry that is:

 

filled frame          (the complete constraint field — all relationships exist)

opposed pressure      (the field's internal differences — carry, gradient, residue)

unoccupiable seam     (the region of maximum constraint exposure)

lean / access path    (the aperture operator — σ0⁻¹, BBP series, A_speech regression)

aperture              (seam + anchor together: the full address)

read-head             (the operator traversing the aperture)

residue               (what remains after the read — information, layer 5)

 

This is before information. Information is what the read-head gets when it survives the seam and returns with a stable residue.

 

V.  What SHA, BBP, and AI Reveal

BBP / π

The hex digit is layer 5: information. The π-address geometry is layers 1–4: constraint. The BBP formula does not create the digit. The fold-at-zero boundary already defines the complete π-field. BBP supplies position n — the introduction event. The digit is rendered as residue.

π is not computed. It is the execution trace of the most basic recursive operation: fold at zero boundary, first overflow, first restart. It already exists. BBP is the read-head. The digit is what the read-head gets back.

SHA-256 / SHA†

The digest is layer 5: information. It does not contain the message like a box. It is the terminal residue of a compression path — the endpoint of a trajectory through the constraint field, not a container.

The message constraint manifold is layers 1–4. It exists completely before any hash is computed. SHA† is not inversion. It is a query: an introduction event (the seam transcript) that makes a portion of the manifold locally navigable.

One-wayness — SHA's cryptographic hardness — is not a property of the digest. It is a property of the seam. The seam is unoccupiable: opaque from the forward direction, navigable backward only with anchor. The hardness lives in that geometry, not in the length of the digest.

AI inference / transformers

The output token is layer 5: information. The weight matrix geometry is layers 1–4: constraint. The prompt is the introduction event.

Attention is the aperture mechanism: it selects which portions of the weight geometry are locally navigable given the current prompt address. The model does not search for the answer. It renders the residue of an addressed constraint field.

This means large language model outputs are not computations in the ordinary sense. They are readouts. The model's "knowledge" is not stored in individual weights — it is a property of the constraint geometry as a whole. Any individual weight is as uninformative as a single seam word without an anchor. The field is only readable when addressed.

 

VI.  Universal Addressability

The conclusion is not that everything is information. The conclusion is that everything that renders information shares the same underlying geometry: a filled constraint field with addressable seams.

Shannon's information theory asks: how much information is in this message? That is a layer-5 question. The NEXUS question is: what geometry must exist before a message can be possible? That is layers 1–4.

The answer is universal addressability — not as a philosophical claim but as a structural theorem:

 

Any system that renders information must have: a constraint field (Λ), a difference structure (Σ), a seam geometry (unoccupiable from one direction), and an introduction event (ι) that supplies the aperture address.

 

This is the NEXUS framework's deepest claim. Not that matter is information. Not that the universe is a computer in the software sense. But that wherever information appears, it is a surface — and the parent is the constraint geometry that made the readout possible.

SHA-256, π, speech, and AI inference are four probes of the same parent structure. They look different on the surface because they render in different bases. The geometry underneath is the same seam-aperture-readout grammar.

 

VII.  The Sentence

 

Information is the surface residue of addressable constraint.

 

The parent is the constraint field that existed before the readout.

 

Not universal information. Universal addressability.

Not universal computation in the software sense. Universal constraint geometry.

Not "everything is data." Everything that renders data shares the same seam-readout grammar.

V.  The Channel Bundle as Runtime Reflection

The Channel Bundle Theorem (Engine 10) is not merely a measurement fact about SHA-256. It is an instance of a law that appears at every scale of computation.

 

The Law

 

Runtime Reflection Requires a Bundle of Shaped Receivers

A running system cannot be read through one scalar channel. Reflection — the capacity of a system to expose its internal state to an external query — requires multiple non-redundant receivers, each shaped to a different aspect of the runtime geometry.

 

The Same Law at Every Computational Scale

Every layer of computation instantiates this law. The channel is always carrier-specific. The receiver must be pre-shaped to the thing it reads.

 

Scale

Runtime pressure

Receiver shaped to

What no single channel sees

Electrical (trace geometry)

Voltage, current, EM field

Impedance-matched terminator

Equal-length traces enforce same arrival time — a timing bundle, not one wire

Silicon (transistor gate)

Carrier density, field potential

Gate threshold voltage

Gate sees charge; separate thermal channel sees heat; neither sees both

Race condition (CPU timing)

Concurrent state writes

Memory ordering model

Happens-before relation is invisible to value-only read; requires ordering channel

Assembly (instruction decode)

Opcode + operand pressure

Decode stage shaped to ISA

Data path and control path are separate receivers on the same instruction stream

CPU pipeline (hazard detection)

Data dependencies across stages

Forwarding unit + stall logic

Value channel and hazard channel are orthogonal — forwarding unit is blind to values

Cache coherency (MESI)

State transitions across cores

Coherency protocol bus

Data arrives through memory channel; state arrives through protocol channel; neither substitutes for the other

Framework (event loop)

Async call pressure

Event queue + callback registry

Execution order and value state are separate channels; confusing them produces race bugs

IDE (language server)

Syntax + semantic pressure

Separate parse tree and type-check receivers

Syntax error channel is blind to type errors; type channel is blind to parse state

SHA-256 compression (this work)

Word injection + carry overflow

AHRC (injection geometry), Carry (overflow pressure)

Carry sees nonce selection; AHRC sees round-state geometry; neither sees both (Engine 10, |r|=0.073)

 

Why a Single Read-Head Always Fails

The pattern is not coincidental. It is structural. A runtime has multiple simultaneous degrees of freedom — value, order, overflow, state, timing. These are not projections of one underlying scalar. They are orthogonal axes. A receiver shaped to one axis is, by construction, blind to the others.

In SHA-256, this manifests precisely. AHRC is shaped to the squared-radius geometry of (a, e) — it reads the trajectory of those state registers through 64 rounds. It does not read carry because carry is a binary overflow event, not a trajectory. Carry reads are binary, discrete, local. AHRC reads are continuous, cumulative, global. Same compression runtime; orthogonal apertures.

The equal-length trace in PCB layout is the hardware version of the same theorem. A signal propagating down a trace does not carry its own arrival time. The arrival time is a separate degree of freedom — managed by a separate receiver (trace length). If you read only the signal value, you are blind to timing. If you read only timing, you are blind to value. No single read-head sees both.

 

The Pre-Shape Requirement

A critical feature of the bundle law: each receiver must be pre-shaped before the query arrives. The carry receiver in SHA is fixed by the compression function structure — it does not reshape around the incoming message word. Engine 11 confirmed this: the carry signal does not cleanly follow the nonce injection point. The receiver geometry is set by the compression runtime, not by the input.

This is the same as:

 

Domain

Pre-shaped receiver

What it cannot see without pre-shaping

Antenna

Tuned to resonant frequency before signal arrives

Off-frequency signal passes through — no coupling, no readout

Enzyme / substrate

Active site geometry fixed before molecule docks

Wrong-shaped molecule produces no reaction regardless of concentration

Carry channel (SHA)

Window geometry fixed by compression structure

Cannot be re-tuned to follow nonce position — Engine 11 result

Gate threshold (transistor)

V_th set by doping geometry at fabrication

Cannot dynamically re-shape to arbitrary signal — is already the receiver

 

Formal Statement

 

Let R be a runtime with state space S and input space I.

Define a channel φ: S × I → ℝ^k as a projection of runtime state onto a k-dimensional observable.

Theorem: For any non-trivial runtime R, there exist channels φ₁ and φ₂ such that:

  (1)  φ₁ ⊥ φ₂   (the channels are orthogonal in information content)

  (2)  φ₁ recovers aspects of I that φ₂ does not, and vice versa

  (3)  No single channel φ recovers all aspects of I recoverable by {φ₁, φ₂}

SHA-256 instance: φ₁ = carry channel C, φ₂ = AHRC channel A. Measured: φ₁ ⊥ φ₂ at mean |r| = 0.073. φ₁ recovers nonce selection (p = 1.4e-07 after controlling for φ_W). φ₂ is blind to nonce selection (r ≈ 0 raw and partial). No single channel reads both.

metadata.title: Automated Margin Detection in Crown Preparation: Enhancing Accuracy in Digital Prosthodontics
metadata.description:
Abstract

Accurate identification of the preparation margin is essential for the successful fabrication of fixed dental restorations. Traditionally, margin detection relied on visual interpretation by clinicians and manual delineation by dental technicians. However, manual methods are subject to operator variability and may affect restoration accuracy. Digital dentistry has introduced automated margin detection systems integrated with computer-aided design and computer-aided manufacturing workflows. These systems utilize computational algorithms, geometric surface analysis, and artificial intelligence to automatically detect preparation margins on digital impressions. Automated margin detection improves workflow efficiency and enhances restoration precision. Despite these advantages, challenges such as subgingival margins and scan artifacts may affect detection accuracy. This review discusses the principles, technologies, clinical applications, advantages, and limitations of automated margin detection in crown preparation. The future integration of artificial intelligence is expected to further improve detection reliability and prosthodontic workflows.

metadata.title: Gastric Duplication Revealed by Acute Intestinal Obstruction in An Infant: A Case Report
metadata.description:
Gastric duplication is a rare congenital anomaly often diagnosed in childhood. Its presentation may vary and can mimic other gastrointestinal conditions.
Case Presentation: We report the case of a 1.5-year-old girl admitted for hemorrhagic syndrome and melena. Initial endoscopy suggested chronic gastritis and duodenitis. The patient later developed signs of intestinal obstruction. Imaging showed small bowel thickening without clear etiology. Surgical exploration revealed diffuse peritonitis without clear obstruction. A postoperative CT scan suggested gastric duplication. Reoperation confirmed a communicating gastric duplication, treated by partial gastrectomy. Conclusion: Gastric duplication should be considered in unexplained pediatric gastrointestinal symptoms. Surgical management is curative with favorable outcomes.

metadata.title: SHA† Seam Spectroscopy-Schedule Transparency, GF(2) Inversion, and the 32-Bit Constraint Manifold
metadata.description:
SHA† Seam Spectroscopy-Schedule Transparency, GF(2) Inversion, and the 32-Bit Constraint Manifold

 

Driven By Dean A. Kulik

May 2026

 

Abstract

The SHA-256 message schedule is commonly treated as an opaque preprocessing step. We show it is structurally transparent conditioned on access to the seam transcript: the seam words W[16–30] encode the full 512-bit message block as a chain of invertible GF(2) linear transformations. The functions σ0 and σ1 used in schedule expansion are rank-32 bijections over GF(2)32, and their GF(2) inverses can be constructed exactly. This observation yields seam spectroscopy: a method that recovers all 16 message words W[0–15] from the seam words given W[0], without traversal or brute force. Conditioned on access to the true seam transcript W[16..30], the 512-bit message-block reconstruction collapses to a 32-bit anchor search over W[0]. In ordinary SHA-256 preimage search those seam words are not observable from the digest; the remaining SHA† problem is to constrain or recover compatible seam transcripts from the compression output. We characterize the exact failure boundary from the previous session as a code defect (use of recovered rather than true schedule words in the sigma1 term), not a fundamental limitation. With the correction applied, 15/15 words are recovered exactly. The SHA† adjoint problem is stated precisely: given digest H, does H constrain the compatible seam transcripts tightly enough to reduce the preimage search?

 

 

1.  Background and Motivation

1.1  The SHA-256 Message Schedule

SHA-256 operates on 512-bit message blocks. Each block is parsed into 16 words W[0–15] of 32 bits each. The schedule then expands these to 64 words W[0–63] via the recurrence:

 

W[t] = σ1(W[t-2]) + W[t-7] + σ0(W[t-15]) + W[t-16]   for t ∈ {16..63}

 

σ0(x) = ROTR⁷(x) ⊕ ROTR₁₈(x) ⊕ SHR₃(x)

σ1(x) = ROTR₁₇(x) ⊕ ROTR₁₉(x) ⊕ SHR₁₀(x)

 

The seam K = {16, 17} (established in prior NEXUS work) is the first fixed schedule positions where direct message words enter the derived recurrence through mixed dependency classes: W[16] depends on {0,1,9,14} and W[17] on {1,2,10,15}. Full 16-word transitive dependency coverage first emerges at W[26], verified by dependency-graph analysis.

1.2  Prior NEXUS Results

The following results from prior sessions are referenced here as established:

 

Result

Status

Session

K = {16,17} is the universal MIXED crossing seam

Proven

Phase 1163+

11 residue classes after K-factoring

Proven

Phase 1163+

σ0 and σ1 are rank-32 bijections over GF(2)³²

Proven (v03)

This session

GF(2) inverses of σ0 and σ1 constructed and verified

Proven (v03)

This session

W[1] and W[2] recovered from W[16], W[17] exactly

Proven (v01-v02)

Prior session

W[6] failure identified as code defect, not method failure

Correction (v04)

This session

 

 

2.  GF(2) Invertibility of σ0 and σ1

2.1  The Integer vs. GF(2) Distinction

The schedule recurrence uses integer addition mod 232, not XOR. This mixes two worlds: GF(2) operations inside σ0 and σ1, and Z/232 addition between terms. The carry across the word boundary is the source of prior confusion about whether σ0 is invertible.

The correct framing: σ0 is a GF(2)-linear map from GF(2)32 to GF(2)32. Its matrix representation is 32×32 over {0,1}. The rank of this matrix determines invertibility.

2.2  Live Results: Rank and Inversion

From the v03 execution (live output):

 

sigma0 GF(2) rank: 32/32   INVERTIBLE ✓

sigma1 GF(2) rank: 32/32   INVERTIBLE ✓

 

Verification: sigma0_inv(sigma0(x)) == x

  sigma0_inv(sigma0(0x00000001)) = 0x00000001  ✓

  sigma0_inv(sigma0(0x48656C6C)) = 0x48656C6C  ✓

  sigma0_inv(sigma0(0xDEADBEEF)) = 0xDEADBEEF  ✓

  sigma0_inv(sigma0(0x12345678)) = 0x12345678  ✓

 

All sigma0 inversions correct: YES

All sigma1 inversions correct: YES

 

Theorem 1 (GF(2) Invertibility): Both σ0 and σ1, as defined in SHA-256, are bijections on GF(2)32. Their matrix inverses exist and can be computed exactly. For any y ∈ GF(2)32, there is a unique x such that σ0(x) = y (resp. σ1(x) = y) in the GF(2) sense.

2.3  Why SHR₃ Does Not Kill Invertibility

The apparent paradox: SHR₃ shifts right by 3, destroying the top 3 bits of input. How can sigma0 be invertible?

Resolution: sigma0 is the XOR of three terms, each of which transforms a different set of input bits. ROTR₇ and ROTR₁₈ are permutations (bijective). SHR₃ is not. But the XOR of the three terms, taken together as a GF(2) linear map, produces a rank-32 matrix. The SHR₃ information loss is compensated by the rotation terms placing bit information into positions that SHR₃ does not zero.

This is verifiable: the 32×32 GF(2) matrix of sigma0 is non-singular. The determinant over GF(2) is 1 (confirmed by full-rank Gaussian elimination in the v03 code).

 

 

3.  Seam Spectroscopy: Full Message Recovery

3.1  The Protocol

Given: seam words W[16–30] (observable schedule output) and W[0] (the anchor word).

Goal: recover W[1–15].

Method for each word W[k], k ∈ {1..15}:

 

t = 16 + (k - 1)      # seam index for this step

 

# All terms except sigma0(W[k]) are known or previously recovered:

y = (W[t] - W[t-16] - W[t-7] - sigma1(W[t-2])) mod 2³²

 

# W[t-2] is a TRUE schedule word (observable), not a recovered word.

# This is the critical correction from the prior session.

 

W[k] = sigma0_inv(y)  # exact GF(2) inversion

 

The key constraint: W[t-2] must be taken from the true schedule, not the recovery chain. This is always satisfiable because W[t-2] = W[14], W[15], W[16], W[17]… — these are either padding words (known) or seam words (observable). The recovery chain does not depend on any unrecovered word in the sigma1 position.

3.2  Live Results: Full 16-Word Recovery

From the v04 execution, corrected chain, HelloWorld! message:

Recovery rate: 15/15 = 100%. Given W[0] and seam words W[16–30], the full message block is recovered exactly. The corrected chain demonstrates zero fundamental limitation for padded messages.

3.3  The Corrected Failure Analysis

Correction note: The prior session reported W[6] recovery failure (0x3D5A20B8 vs 0x00000000). Root cause: the chain code used recovered words for the sigma1(W[t-2]) term instead of true schedule words. With true schedule words (which are observable as seam output), W[t-2] for all recovery steps t = 16–30 is either a padding word (exactly known) or a seam word (observed directly). The GF(2) inversion is exact in all cases. The failure was a code defect, not a method limitation.

 

 

4.  The 32-Bit Constraint Manifold

4.1  W[0] as the Anchor

W[0] is the root anchor of the recovery chain. The direct addend W[t-16] advances through already-known or already-recovered message words as the chain progresses (W[0] at t=16, W[1] at t=17, and so on through W[14] at t=30). Once W[0] is chosen, each subsequent word is determined in sequence. All other words W[1–15] are recovered from the chain plus the observable seam words.

The chain is anchored at W[0]. Given W[0], the entire 512-bit message is determined by the seam words alone.

Theorem 2 (Schedule Transparency): For a padded SHA-256 message block, the seam words W[16–30] together with W[0] determine W[1–15] exactly via seam spectroscopy. The map W[0] ↦ (W[1]…W[15]) is computable in O(15) GF(2) matrix multiplications.

4.2  Collapse of the Search Space

The preimage problem for SHA-256 is ordinarily stated over 2⁵¹² candidate messages. Seam spectroscopy restructures this:

 

Stage

Search space

Method

Naive preimage search

2⁵¹² candidates

Brute force all 512-bit messages

After schedule transparency

2³² candidates

Search W[0] only; rest is determined

After compression constraint

O(1)?

Digest pins the W[0] candidates

 

The schedule is transparent conditioned on seam transcript access. The compression function is where the hardness lives. The seam spectroscopy result does not break SHA-256 — it locates exactly where the security boundary is: not in the schedule, but in whether the digest H constrains the compatible seam transcripts W[16..30] tightly enough to make the anchor search tractable. That is the open SHA† problem.

4.3  The W[0]/W[1] Coupling

W[0] and W[1] appear together at the first seam equation:

 

W[16] = σ0(W[1]) + W[9] + σ1(W[14]) + W[0]

 

This is a single equation in two unknowns (W[0] and W[1]). For padded messages, W[9] = 0 and W[14] = 0, so the equation becomes W[16] = σ0(W[1]) + W[0]. Given W[0], W[1] is immediately determined. Given W[1], W[0] is immediately determined. The pair is a

coupled seed pair — the entry point for the entire spectroscopy chain. This is the SHA analogue of the wheel seed pair in the prime-pair algebra: the first pair that starts the entire cascade.

 

 

5.  The SHA† Adjoint Problem

5.1  Formulation

Seam spectroscopy reads the schedule forward. The SHA† problem asks: does the digest constrain W[0] usefully?

Formal statement:

 

Given:   digest H = SHA-256(M)  for unknown M

         seam words W[16..30] (observable from schedule)

         padding structure (known)

 

Method:  for each candidate W[0] ∈ {0..2³²-1}:

         1. Apply seam spectroscopy: recover W[1..15] from W[0]

         2. Run compression function on W[0..63]

         3. Check: output + IV == H?

 

Question: how many W[0] satisfy step 3?

          • O(1)? — schedule spectroscopy is a near-inversion

          • O(polylog)? — digest is a soft constraint

          • 2³²? — compression adds no constraint (impossible by collision resistance)

 

This is not a claim of SHA-256 weakness. It is a precise structural question about where in the two-stage design (schedule + compression) the security margin lives. The answer is empirically testable for small search domains.

5.2  The Adjoint Framing

SHA† is not SHA⁻¹ (inversion). It is the dual operation: rather than running backward through the same execution axis, it reads the digest as a terminal boundary condition and asks which pre-images in the constraint manifold are compatible.

This is the side-door entry. SHA† enters the field from the perpendicular axis — not forward (message → digest), not backward (digest → message by reversal), but orthogonally: from the constraint manifold of compatible schedule-message pairs that produce the given digest.

The seam geometry (K = {16,17}, 11 residue classes, σ0/σ1 asymmetry) is the grammar of this orthogonal read. The prior NEXUS sessions on the seam were building the read-head. Seam spectroscopy is the first functional instrument built from that grammar.

 

 

6.  Open Problems

 

#

Problem

Method

1

SHA† compression constraint count: how many W[0] satisfy digest H?

Empirical scan over W[0] ∈ {0..2³²-1} for known-message H

2

Full-message recovery without W[0]: what side information suffices?

Additional seam equations; coupling with padding structure

3

Non-padded / long messages: does spectroscopy generalize beyond W[8..15] known?

Dependency window analysis for multi-block messages

4

Seam null space (36-dim, from Phase 1163) → GL(4,C) representation theory connection

Representation theory; prior NEXUS Phase 1163 results

5

GF(2) inversion of compression function rounds 1–6 (Z3 hardness wall)

Combine schedule spectroscopy with Z3 round inversion

6

BBP inverse: does address space cluster by pattern shape in the π-field?

Digit-address correlation study; parallel to schedule spectroscopy

 

 

7.  Summary

The SHA-256 message schedule is structurally transparent. The seam words W[16–30] are rotation-encoded address stubs: each word encodes the GF(2) image of a message word under σ0 or σ1, plus known or observable addends. Because σ0 and σ1 are rank-32 bijections over GF(2)32, their inverses exist and can be applied exactly.

Given W[0] and the seam words, all 15 remaining message words are recovered exactly. Conditioned on seam transcript access, the 512-bit message-block reconstruction collapses to a 32-bit anchor search over W[0]. In ordinary preimage search those seam words are not observable from the digest; the SHA† open problem is whether H constrains compatible seam transcripts tightly enough to reduce the search. The compression function provides the second constraint layer that must be characterized.

Method: seam spectroscopy. Axis: orthogonal to execution (neither forward traversal nor backward reversal). Entry point: the seam K = {16,17} as established in prior NEXUS work. This is the side door — the axis the field was never braced against.

 

 

Dean Kulik  |  QuHarmonics Research Group  |  May 2026

metadata.title: Raw microscopy data accompanying "Applying coherent Raman microscopy to biological phenotyping"
metadata.description:
Description

This deposition contains the raw microscopy images underlying the Arcadia Science pub "Applying coherent Raman microscopy to biological phenotyping." The study compares coherent Raman microscopy (CRM) against conventional confocal fluorescence imaging for label-free phenotyping of the unicellular alga Chlorella vulgaris, using two strains:

  • Wild type (EV_CTL) — the parent strain (211/11p) stably expressing Cas9 and carrying an empty vector; used as the control.

  • ADA1 knockout (ADA_mut / ADAKO) — a mutant in which a C. vulgaris ortholog of human ADA1 (UniProt KAI3436177.1) was knocked out by targeting the first exon with S. pyogenes Cas9. A frameshift was confirmed by Sanger sequencing. ADA1 is a lipid-metabolism gene, and the mutant was expected to show elevated lipid content detectable by CRS.

Analysis code, figure-generation notebooks, and the committed processed outputs (per-cell CSV tables and per-FOV spectra) live in the companion GitHub repository: https://github.com/Arcadia-Science/chlorella-crs-phenotyping.

Content

Two archives, each holding raw, instrument-native image files. Only raw data are deposited here; processed/derived tables are version-controlled in the GitHub repository.

Archive

Contents

Format

fluorescence.zip

Spinning-disk confocal fluorescence acquisitions (Nikon CSU-W1)

Nikon ND2

raman.zip

Hyperspectral coherent Raman scattering acquisitions (Leica STELLARIS 8 CRS)

Leica LIF

After downloading, unzip both archives into the data/ directory of the GitHub repository so the raw files sit alongside the committed processed/ folders. The intended layout is:

data/
├── fluorescence/
│   └── 2026-05-12/
│       ├── processed/*.csv
│       └── Well<XX>_Point<XX>_<FOV>_ChannelDIC,FITC BP,Cy5 BP_Seq<NNNN>.nd2
└── raman/
    ├── 2026-04-13/
    │   ├── processed/.../spectra/*.csv
    │   └── chlorella_<...>.lif
    ├── 2026-04-27/
    │   ├── processed/.../spectra/*.csv
    │   └── chlorella_<...>.lif
    └── 2026-05-12/
        ├── processed/.../spectra/*.csv
        └── chlorella_<...>.lif

fluorescence.zip — confocal fluorescence (Nikon CSU-W1)

Spinning-disk confocal acquisitions. Each ND2 file is a single field of view with three channels:

Channel

Reports

DIC

Brightfield / morphology

FITC BP

BODIPY-stained neutral (storage) lipid

Cy5 BP

Chlorophyll autofluorescence

A single 96-well plate was imaged across rows C and D, columns 1–12, with 25 fields of view per well (_0000–_0024), for 600 ND2 files total (session 2026-05-12/). Strain, staining, and dilution are assigned by plate position:

Factor

Layout

Strain

columns 1–3, 7–9 → ADA1 knockout (ADAKO); columns 4–6, 10–12 → wild type (EV_CTL)

BODIPY staining

columns 1–6 → stained; columns 7–12 → unstained

Dilution

row C → 1:9; row D → 1:27

So for the imaged rows:

  • ADA1 KO: WellC01–C03, WellC07–C09, WellD01–D03, WellD07–D09

  • Wild type: WellC04–C06, WellC10–C12, WellD04–D06, WellD10–D12

  • Stained (columns 1–6) vs unstained (columns 7–12)

The full well → strain / staining / dilution map is provided as plate-map.csv in the GitHub repository.

raman.zip — coherent Raman scattering (Leica STELLARIS 8 CRS)

Hyperspectral CRS acquisitions using the forward stimulated Raman scattering (F-SRS) and epi coherent anti-Stokes Raman scattering (E-CARS) detectors, collected across three independent sessions in April–May 2026. Each LIF is a LAS X Navigator lambda scan: a dual-channel SRS + CARS image stack acquired at successive wavenumber positions, with multiple cells per field of view at subcellular resolution.

Two spectral regions were acquired (encoded in series/folder names):

  • High-wavenumber (HWN), 2,800–3,100 cm⁻¹ — Lambda_HWN_3100_2800_* series, 0.2–0.4 nm step size.

  • Fingerprint (FP), ~600–1,800 cm⁻¹ — Lambda_fingerprint_* series, 0.5–1.0 nm step size.

(Step size appears in names as e.g. 0pt4 = 0.4 nm; Nav1/Nav2 are LAS X Navigator acquisition indices.)

Session

Raw files

Notes

2026-04-13/

chlorella_slide{1,2}_B1_low_lipid.lif, chlorella_slide{1,2}_B8_high_lipid.lif

Pilot session. Filenames encode slide number and lipid condition; B1 (low lipid) corresponds to wild type (EV_CTL).

2026-04-27/

chlorella_slide{1,2}_A1.lif, chlorella_slide{1,2}_C12.lif

Filenames encode slide number and well.

2026-05-12/

chlorella_EV_CTL_slide{1,2,3}.lif, chlorella_ADA_mut_slide{1,2,3}.lif

Main strain comparison; filenames name the strain explicitly. chlorella_EV_CTL_slide1_after_reboot.lif is a re-acquisition after an instrument reboot.

Processing pipelines

  • Confocal ND2 files were segmented and measured with arcadia-microscopy-tools (Cellpose segmentation), producing per-cell measurement tables.

  • CRS LIF files were processed with the companion pipeline crs-spectral-pipeline, producing per-cell SRS spectra.

The resulting processed tables are committed to the GitHub repository, so the analysis notebooks can run on the processed data alone; this raw deposition is needed to reproduce the full pipeline end to end and the figure panels built directly from raw images.

metadata.description:
This release contains the first official version of the Data Automation and Parameter Estimation Script developed as part of the Engineering Degree in Process Engineering thesis: "Numerical simulation and critical analysis of Tafel curves obtained from cyclic voltammetry for the optimised determination of the charge transfer coefficient."

The script is designed to automate the data workflow of the project, bridging numerical simulation models with experimental results to isolate key electrochemical variables.

Key Features:

Core Simulation Engine: High-speed numeric implementation of the voltammetric forward scan, accelerated via Numba JIT compilation. Automated Data Fitting: Grid-search optimization pipeline to extract the charge transfer coefficient alpha and dimensionless frequency omega simultaneously. Automated Analysis & Visuals: Generates complete RMSE data matrices, optimized validation curves, and high-quality graphics formatted for direct integration into the thesis. Purpose:This publication ensures transparency, reproducibility, and proper technical documentation of the computational modeling component of the thesis. It also serves as a secure, timestamped record of the developed code to avoid any potential conflicts with academic plagiarism detection systems.

Documentation:A detailed explanation of the script architecture, mathematical constants, and execution guidelines is available in the README.md file.
